title: Double-Byte Character Strings
text: Double-Byte Character Strings Multibyte character sets are available for use with Uniface. Only connectors for databases that support double-byte data need to be changed. CAUTION: When you apply multibyte capabilities, ensure that connectors for non double-byte databases do not receive double-byte data. The connector is responsible for converting the internal character set strings when communicating with the DBMS. This conversion happens, for example, when the connector creates a logon string during the Logon request to connect to the DBMS. The connector is also responsible for converting from the target character set to the internal character set when it is communicating to the kernel. This happens, for example, when the connector passes a DBMS-specific error message to the kernel in the Message request. The key for enabling double-byte capability in the connector is the Uniface string library. This is a subset of the Uniface run-time library, containing a number of routines that you can use to manipulate double-byte strings. These routines take care of the difference between single-byte and multibyte data in a transparent manner. The main effort for the connector developer lies in identifying the places in the connector where these routines need to be used, that is, all places where (potentially double-byte) information passes between the Uniface kernel and the database. In these places, you must use routines from the Uniface generic string library to convert between the Uniface internal charter set and the external character set as defined by the database. The routines ucnvext() and ucnvint() are described in the file unidbm.h . In all these cases, you should supply a double-byte sized buffer. It is recommended that you align the buffer on an eight-byte memory boundary. Generally, you should include the following line at the beginning of the connector: #include "unidbm.h" Copy It is recommended that you leave the old code intact and call the new code only if a double-byte assignment setting for the connector is in effect. The table shows the character sets necessary for multibyte enabling: Table 1. Character sets used per request for multibyte assignments Calls uname ufstring sqlbuf umisc iobuf uiflist Relationshiptype Logoff I Logon I I I Open I I I I Name Table I I I I I Close I I I Select I I I I T T Fetch I I T T Write I I I T T Update I I I T T Delete I I I T Wildcard I I I T Commit I Rollback I Info I Message I I SQL I I Related Links Packing Codes for Unicode By default, the W packing code is for Unicode characters. The U* packing code can also be used for holding Unicode characters. If the entity character set is Unicode, the C packing code can also be used for Unicode characters. Developing International Applications Uniface supports Unicode and many other character sets, which makes it possible to develop international applications in different languages. It enables you to take locale into account when displaying, sorting, and manipulating data, and to adjust times to the client time zone. It also allows you to configure an application for right-to-left languages. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
